A living record of Sonoran Desert biodiversity.
A seed harvester ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus) running across a piece of charcoal near a campfire in the Superstition Wilderness.
A desert spiny lizard basking on a boulder at Miles Ranch Trailhead.

An ever-growing record of the remarkable diversity of life across the Sonoran Desert. Built from thousands of field observations over more than a decade, the Compendium connects species, habitats, and ecological interactions into a freely accessible resource for naturalists, educators, and the endlessly curious.

An intimate experiment in transforming an urban yard into a haven for native plants and animals.
A potter wasp sleeps atop the dried stalk of a plant in the yard.

What happens when a suburban yard is designed for wildlife instead of lawns? For more than a decade, we have been working together to transform our suburban yard into a refuge for the native plants and wildlife of our home county. What began as a shared interest in gardening has grown into a long-term collaboration in ecological restoration, guided by the belief that even small urban spaces can make a meaningful difference for biodiversity. Today, our landscape is home to more than 100 plant species native to Maricopa County, Arizona, creating habitat for native bees, butterflies, birds, reptiles, and countless other species that depend on these plants to survive. One of the most rewarding aspects of this journey has been watching life return. Year after year, we have carefully documented the plants, insects, birds, and other wildlife that have found their way to our garden as it has matured. Every new species is a reminder that restoring native habitat is an ongoing conversation with the natural world—one that unfolds through patience, observation, and a willingness to let nature lead. Our garden continues to teach us that when native plants thrive, entire ecological communities can begin to recover. We are passionate about sharing what we've learned and encouraging others to see their own landscapes as opportunities for conservation. Our garden was featured in [Zygote Quarterly](https://zqjournal.org/pdfs/ZQ_issue_39.pdf) for its ecological design and as an example of the beauty and fulfillment that come from creating spaces for native species. It has also been certified by the [Maricopa Pollinator Pathway Garden Certification Program](https://libguides.maricopa.edu/seed/pathway) in recognition of its value as pollinator habitat. We hope our work inspires others to discover that conservation can begin right outside the front door, one native plant at a time.

An open reference for identifying North America's small mammals.

Combining detailed identification resources with structured data, [WhiskerWiki](https://whiskerwiki.org) helps researchers, wildlife professionals, and curious naturalists navigate one of the continent's most overlooked groups of mammals.

WhiskerWiki is a collaborative, open-access resource dedicated to the identification, taxonomy, natural history, and distribution of the shrews, moles, and rodents of the United States. Designed for field biologists, museum professionals, students, researchers, and naturalists, the site brings together current taxonomic research, diagnostic characteristics, species accounts, identification keys, range maps, and county- and state-level species lists in a single, searchable platform. The project emphasizes scientific accuracy while acknowledging that mammal taxonomy is continually evolving, making WhiskerWiki both a practical identification guide and a living resource for the small mammal community.

Built as a community-driven knowledge base, WhiskerWiki encourages collaboration among researchers and enthusiasts to improve species accounts, document new discoveries, and refine our understanding of small mammals in the United States. By making authoritative information freely available and easy to navigate, the project aims to support biodiversity research, museum collections, wildlife surveys, education, and conservation while fostering a deeper appreciation for the remarkable diversity of often-overlooked mammals.

Building knowledge, tools, and stories that deepen our understanding of biodiversity.
Through photography, artwork, open data, software, and long-term field projects, we create resources that inspire curiosity and make the natural world more accessible.

Oversteger Studios is an independent natural history studio created by [Rick Overson](https://rickoverson.com) and [Laura Steger](https://laurasteger.com).

We believe that careful observation, thoughtful organization, and open sharing can help more people discover and understand biodiversity. Through software development, photography, scientific illustration, open data, and long-term field projects, we create freely available resources that make the natural world more accessible.

Each project is built around the same idea: to observe, document, organize, and share knowledge in ways that are useful to naturalists, educators, researchers, and anyone curious about the living world.

Whether it’s a species reference, a field photograph, a dataset, or a software tool, our goal is always the same—to create resources that people can use, learn from, and return to.

We build tools and stories that help people see and understand the living world.

## Contact

We welcome collaborations, data sharing, project ideas, corrections, and conversations with fellow naturalists, educators, researchers, photographers, and anyone curious about the natural world.

Whether you’ve spotted an error, have a question about a project, or are interested in working together, we’d love to hear from you.
